analysis:
- Common pipeline: all stronger heuristics follow a three‑stage pattern — (1) nondominated prefilter, (2) build an adaptive shortlist using cheap proxies (box/rect volume and weight‑vector scalarizations), (3) construct the k‑set with a greedy/CELF lazy greedy using exact hypervolume (HV) calls, then (4) bounded local 1‑for‑1 swaps and deterministic padding to exactly k.
- Proxy vs exact tradeoff: proxies (rect volumes, scalarized scores, many weight vectors) reduce HV calls but vary in fidelity and cost (more weights -> better HVC approx but greater precompute cost). Several variants tune M, shortlist_size, and blending exponents to trade precision ↔ runtime.
- CELF lazy greedy and singleton caching appear across top methods — effective because singleton HVs are cheap to cache and timestamps avoid recomputing many marginals. Selective pairwise caches accelerate marginal recompute when shortlist is small.
- HV-call management: better heuristics explicitly cap or budget HV calls and use cheap upper bounds (rect_vol, singleton HV) to prune candidates; weaker ones either do many full HV recomputations or rely on heavy random shuffles (less reproducible).
- Local search: bounded deterministic 1‑for‑1 swaps (with caching/pruning) consistently improve solutions with controlled cost; unlimited exhaustive swaps (or repeated full HV recompute loops) are the main runtime sink.
- Determinism and tie-breaking: top variants fix RNG seeds, deterministic sorts/tie breaks and stable padding — important for reproducible comparisons.
- Efficiency observations: vectorized nondominated filters and proxy computations scale well; O(N^2) dominance checks and unbounded pairwise HV caching blow up on larger N. Precomputing too many pairwise HVs can waste budget; selective caching (shortlist size threshold) hits the sweet spot.
- Robustness: good heuristics guard against small numerical issues (eps, clamp), validate reference_point dim, and ensure exactly k outputs with deterministic padding. Some weaker variants miss strict input checks or mix randomness without fixed seeds.
experience: Favor adaptive shortlists driven by pareto size/diversity, use cheap box+scalar proxies to prune, then run CELF with cached singleton HVs and selective pairwise caching (only when shortlist ≤ threshold). Enforce an HV‑call budget, apply tight upper‑bound pruning, and use bounded deterministic 1‑for‑1 swaps. Tune weight counts and shortlist size adaptively (not fixed), exploit vectorization/parallelism, and prefer incremental or library‑level HV updates to minimize expensive recomputations.
current_reflection: Favor adaptive shortlists driven by pareto size/diversity, use cheap box+scalar proxies to prune, then run CELF with cached singleton HVs and selective pairwise caching (only when shortlist ≤ threshold). Enforce an HV‑call budget, apply tight upper‑bound pruning, and use bounded deterministic 1‑for‑1 swaps. Tune weight counts and shortlist size adaptively (not fixed), exploit vectorization/parallelism, and prefer incremental or library‑level HV updates to minimize expensive recomputations.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Favor a compact, Pareto-aware shortlist (O(k)), CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton HVs and selective pairwise caching, and very aggressive cheap upper‑bound pruning (box- and scalar-based) to avoid HV calls. Use vectorized nondominated filtering, sets/heap for O(1)/O(log n) ops, an HV-call budget, and incremental swaps restricted to top candidates. Keep determinism by score-based tie-breaks; only inject controlled randomness if necessary.

Design pipelines that short‑circuit expensive HV calls: vectorize Pareto and proxy scoring, shortlist O(k) candidates, run CELF with singleton (and selective pairwise) caches, prune with box‑volume upper bounds, and limit exact HV swaps with a small deterministic budget. This yields reproducible, fast, and high‑quality k‑subsets without exploding HV computations.

Prefer a small, k‑proportional shortlist driven by mixed box+few‑weight scores; use CELF with singleton (and selective pairwise) caching plus timestamping; prune candidates via box‑volume upper bounds and cap HV calls; vectorize nondominated filtering and hotspot ops; avoid dense weight banks—use adaptive sampling; and ensure deterministic tie‑breaks. These keep HV calls low while preserving solution quality.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto shortlist; box+scalar proxies; CELF; singleton & selective pairwise HV cache; HV-call budget; upper‑bound pruning; bounded deterministic 1‑for‑1 swaps; adaptive shortlist/weights; vectorization; incremental HV updates
- Advice: Prefilter dominated points, build an adaptive shortlist using mixed box and few-weight proxies, run CELF with cached singleton HVs and selective pairwise caching (only when shortlist ≤ threshold), enforce a strict HV-call budget, apply tight upper‑bound pruning, use bounded deterministic swaps, and exploit vectorized/incremental HV library updates.
- Avoid: Nested or repeated exact HV recomputations, dense weight banks, per-call Python overhead, and uncontrolled randomness; prefer batching, timestamping, and deterministic tie‑breaks.
- Explanation: These measures keep exact HV evaluations low, preserve solution quality, and ensure reproducible, fast k‑subset selection.
